title: Lemon and poppyseed cake
subtitle: Zesty loaf cake with a sharp lemon drizzle
excerpt: The lemon drizzle goes on while the cake is still hot and in the tin — the sugar soaks in rather than sitting on top, which keeps the crust crisp. Poppyseeds add texture without flavour; the lemon does all the work.
sourceNotes: Lemon drizzle cake became a fixture of British café culture and domestic baking in the late twentieth century. The loaf tin form with a sugar-lemon drizzle applied hot is the standard British version, widely documented in domestic cookbooks and magazine recipes from the 1980s onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced and wrapped. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: This loaf is excellent the next day. Store in an airtight tin at room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: The method of pouring a liquid sugar-and-citrus mixture over a cake while it is still hot and in its tin. The heat opens up the pores in the crumb and allows the drizzle to soak in rather than running off the surface. Applied correctly, it creates a sweet-sharp crust along the top and edges as the sugar crystallises while cooling.

content.text: The drizzle is what separates this from a plain lemon cake. The

requires speed — as soon as the cake comes out of the oven, use a skewer to poke holes all over the top, then pour the lemon-sugar mixture over immediately. Waiting for the cake to cool first means the drizzle sits on top rather than soaking in.

attrs.prepNote: zest only, finely grated — use unwaxed

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Line a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin with baking paper. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Add the {{lemon}} zest. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}}, then stir in the {{poppy-seeds}} and {{whole-milk}}. Scrape into the tin and bake for 40–45 minutes until a skewer comes out clean.

Stir the drizzle {{lemon-juice}} and {{caster-sugar}} together in a small bowl — don't heat it. The moment the cake comes out of the oven, poke it all over with a skewer, going right to the bottom. Pour the drizzle slowly and evenly over the surface. Leave in the tin until completely cool. The drizzle needs time to set into the crust before the cake is moved.
